title: Conceptual Aircraft Sizing Study
description: Turn a mission brief into a first-pass aircraft concept by sizing weight, wing area, and installed propulsion for a regional or utility aircraft.
whyItMatters: Early sizing decisions shape everything downstream. If the concept does not close on weight, runway, climb, and range at the conceptual stage, detailed design will only make the problem more expensive.
skillsLearned: Wing loading and power loading analysis

steps.title: Define the mission
steps.description: Write a one-page requirement set covering payload, range, cruise speed, runway length, reserve policy, and operating environment.
steps.tips: Keep the mission specific. A vague aircraft brief leads to vague sizing results.

steps.title: Estimate takeoff mass
steps.description: Use payload, crew, fuel fraction, and empty-weight assumptions to solve for takeoff gross weight using a conceptual sizing loop.
steps.tips: Capture every assumption in a simple table so you can iterate quickly.

steps.title: Build constraint charts
steps.description: Plot wing loading against power or thrust loading for takeoff, landing, climb, cruise, and stall constraints.
steps.tips: The best concept point usually sits with some margin, not directly on every limit line.

steps.title: Translate the concept into geometry
steps.description: Convert the chosen design point into wing area, aspect ratio, propulsion size, and a rough configuration sketch.
steps.tips: Use OpenVSP or a hand sketch first. The goal is coherent sizing, not polished CAD.

steps.title: Review the trade
steps.description: Summarise what drives the concept most strongly and what assumptions would most likely force a redesign.
steps.tips: Sensitivity analysis is more valuable than pretending the first answer is exact.

deliverables: Constraint chart with chosen design point
Rough aircraft geometry or OpenVSP model
Short design review note on major risks and trade-offs

rubric.description: Requirements are specific, measurable, and internally consistent.
Mass, wing loading, and propulsion choices follow from the assumptions and constraint analysis.
The work clearly explains what the design gains and gives up.
Plots, assumptions, and conclusions are easy to review.
